name: Agentic Compute Criticality / Agent Scaling Laws (2025)
origin_domain: AI research (large language model scaling laws, agentic systems engineering)
year_developed: 2025 (Sivamani Battala independent research, Zenodo DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19469219; Google DeepMind + MIT arXiv:2512.08296, December 2025)
progenitor: Sivamani Battala (independent researcher, India) — Agentic Compute Criticality model; Google DeepMind + MIT team — 'Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems' (180 controlled configurations across 3 LLM families, 5 architectures, 4 benchmarks)
driving_insight: Traditional LLM scaling laws (Performance = f(Parameters, Data, Training_Compute)) fail to predict the behavior of agentic systems because they omit inference-time recursion. When an LLM agent spawns sub-agents, verifies outputs, and chains recursive reasoning, compute cost follows a branching process whose stability depends entirely on the branching factor b. If b < 1, expected operations = 1/(1-b) — stable and predictable. If b >= 1, expected operations diverge to infinity. The Google/MIT paper empirically confirms this: adding agents to tasks where a single agent already achieves >45% accuracy produces NEGATIVE returns (beta = -0.408). The new scaling dimension is Inference_Depth, capturing recursive execution chains that multiply compute far beyond what parameter count alone predicts.
decision_style: algorithmic with empirical validation — the branching factor model is a mathematical framework (geometric branching process), while the Google/MIT scaling laws are empirically derived from 180 controlled experiments. Together they form a predictive framework (87% accuracy) for determining BEFORE deployment whether a task should use single-agent or multi-agent architecture.
problem_class_targeted: any problem where agentic AI systems (single or multi-agent) are deployed, with the specific goal of predicting whether agentic orchestration will HELP or HARM performance. Targeted at: software engineering tasks (SWE-bench), web browsing, finance analysis, Minecraft planning, and general tool-use tasks. The framework distinguishes three task types: parallelizable (multi-agent helps), exploratory (multi-agent neutral-to-slightly-helps), and sequential/dependent (multi-agent HARMS, -39% to -70% degradation).
ordered_phases: The framework does not prescribe a process but provides a decision protocol: (1) Measure the single-agent baseline accuracy on the target task; (2) Assess task topology (parallelizable vs. sequential vs. exploratory); (3) Apply the 45% Rule — if single-agent accuracy > 45%, adding agents is counterproductive; (4) Apply the Golden Rule — never exceed 3-4 agents; (5) For multi-agent: choose topology (centralized for parallelizable, decentralized for exploratory); (6) Monitor communication rounds — if rounds exceed 7x the single-agent baseline, halt and fall back to single-agent.
feedback_loops: The feedback loop is diagnostic, not operational: the framework predicts that beyond certain thresholds (b > 1, agents > 4, single-agent accuracy > 45%), performance degrades. This forms a negative feedback signal that should trigger an architectural rollback (revert to fewer agents or single-agent). The Google/MIT paper identifies 'coordination collapse' as an emergent feedback loop — agents correcting each other's outputs create compounding errors rather than improvements.
gates: Implicit decision gates: (1) Branching factor check — if estimated b >= 1, block full agentic deployment; (2) Single-agent accuracy gate — if >45%, block multi-agent design; (3) Topology gate — if task is sequential/dependent, block multi-agent; (4) Communication round gate — if rounds > 7x baseline, trigger rollback. These are analytical gates derived from the empirical scaling laws, not runtime software gates.
exit_criteria: The framework is a predictive/analytical tool, not an execution system. Exit criteria apply to the ANALYSIS phase: (1) task characterized across all three scaling laws; (2) optimal agent count and topology determined; (3) estimated branching factor b < 1 confirmed. For deployed agent systems informed by this framework: exit when the task is complete OR when runtime metrics (rounds, cost) exceed predicted thresholds, triggering fallback.
decomposition_method: Not a decomposition method per se — the framework ANALYZES decomposition trade-offs. The key insight is that decomposition (splitting work across agents) creates a branching process with factor b = (average sub-tasks spawned per step). The framework provides the mathematical tools to determine whether a given decomposition strategy will be stable (b < 1) or explosive (b >= 1). The Google/MIT paper's Law 1 (Tool-Coordination Trade-off) directly addresses when decomposition across tools/agents becomes self-defeating.
granularity_guidance: The 45% Rule provides granularity guidance: if the base agent already handles a sub-task with >45% accuracy, do NOT further decompose that sub-task — the coordination overhead exceeds the benefit. For tool-heavy tasks (>16 tools), use a single agent rather than multiple. The Golden Rule caps agent count at 3-4, implying that decomposition granularity must respect this ceiling.
boundary_rules: Topology-dependent boundary rules: for parallelizable tasks, boundaries should be clean (no dependencies); for sequential tasks, boundaries are forced by dependency chains and multi-agent decomposition is CONTRAINDICATED; for exploratory tasks, boundaries can be fuzzy but agents must not share decision authority (decentralized debate, not centralized voting). The framework strongly warns against tight coupling across agent boundaries — this creates the n-squared communication links that drive the quadratic round growth (exponent ~1.7).
risk_assessment_mechanism: The framework IS a risk assessment mechanism. It predicts three specific failure modes quantitatively: (1) Compute explosion — branching factor b >= 1 causes exponential cost growth; (2) Coordination collapse — >16 tools or >4 agents causes communication overhead to dominate; (3) Capability saturation — single-agent accuracy >45% makes multi-agent harmful (beta = -0.408). The 87% prediction accuracy provides a confidence measure. Risk is scored by distance from the critical thresholds.
unknown_handling: For tasks where the topology is unknown or the single-agent baseline has not been measured, the framework defaults to the safest architecture: single-agent. This is the key recommendation — 'when in doubt, use fewer agents.' The Google/MIT paper provides heuristics for assessing task topology from task descriptions alone, but these are not validated as a standalone capability.
default_stance_on_unknown: RESTRICTIVE — the framework defaults to the simplest, safest architecture (single-agent or minimal multi-agent) unless there is empirical evidence that more agents help. This is the core message: 'stop blindly building AI swarms.' The framework explicitly warns against the optimistic assumption that more agents = better performance.
safety_guardrails: The framework identifies but does not implement safety guardrails. Key safety-relevant findings: (1) Independent (no coordination) multi-agent architectures amplify errors by 17.2x — this is the most dangerous topology; (2) Centralized manager-review architectures are safest (4.4x error amplification); (3) The 3-4 agent ceiling functions as an implicit safety guardrail — beyond this, cascading failures dominate; (4) The framework recommends monitoring communication rounds as a runtime safety signal (halt if >7x baseline).
human_decision_points: Humans decide: (1) whether to apply the framework's predictions (architect choice); (2) what single-agent baseline to use; (3) whether to override the framework's recommendation based on domain knowledge. During execution: humans should monitor communication round counts and cost metrics, halting if they exceed predicted thresholds. The human is ON the loop (monitoring) not IN the loop (per-action approval).
override_capability: Humans can override at the architectural level (choose a different agent count/topology than the framework recommends) and at the runtime level (halt execution, switch to single-agent fallback). Override granularity: architectural (full topology change) or runtime (full halt + restart with different config). No per-subtask override — the framework operates at the architecture level.
fallback_when_no_human: The framework provides automated fallback guidance: if runtime metrics exceed thresholds (rounds > 7x baseline, cost acceleration detected), the system should auto-fallback to single-agent mode. This is a design recommendation, not an implemented mechanism in the framework itself. Without human monitoring, the framework's predictions must be encoded as automated guardrails.
max_problem_size: The Google/MIT paper tested across 4 benchmarks with tasks involving up to 16+ tools. The Battala research demonstrated: baseline operations = 1.0 (8.5M compute units), agentic operations = 6.1 (38.7M compute, 4.5x amplification), peak amplification = 21x. Theoretical limit: any problem where b < 1 is tractable; problems where b approaches 1 become unpredictably expensive. What breaks at scale: agent count > 3-4, tool count > 16, single-agent baseline > 45%.
parallel_execution: Parallelism is analyzed as a double-edged sword. For parallelizable tasks, centralized multi-agent architectures achieve +80.9% gains. However, parallelism creates communication overhead: n agents create n^2 communication links and near-quadratic round growth (exponent ~1.7). The framework's topology analysis determines whether a task's parallelism benefits outweigh the communication penalty. Independent parallel agents without coordination amplify errors by 17.2x — the worst-case topology.
team_scaling_model: NEGATIVE after threshold (3-4 agents). Single-agent baseline: 7.2 rounds average. Multi-agent mixed: 44.3 rounds (6.2x more). The 45% Rule: beta = -0.408 for tasks where single-agent > 45%. For parallelizable tasks, scaling is positive up to ~3 agents then plateaus. For sequential tasks, scaling is NEGATIVE at 2+ agents (-39% to -70% at 4 agents). This is super-linear degradation beyond the threshold.
cost_scaling_model: Two mathematical models converge: (1) Battala's branching process: Expected operations = 1/(1-b), cost scales geometrically as b approaches 1. For b = 0.84, expected = 6.25 (matches empirical 6.1-6.4). (2) Google/MIT's communication model: cost scales approximately as O(n^2) in agent count due to communication links, with round growth exponent ~1.7. Combined: total cost = base_cost * 1/(1-b) * n^alpha where alpha ~ 1.7 for multi-agent. Stable when b < 1 AND n <= 4.
replanning_built_in: The framework recommends architectural replanning (switch topology, reduce agent count) when runtime metrics violate predicted thresholds. This is an architectural replan, not a task-level replan. The framework itself does not implement replanning — it provides the decision criteria for when to replan. In the Google/MIT framework, the recommended replanning trigger is communication rounds exceeding 7x the single-agent baseline.
trigger_mechanism: Architectural-level triggers: (1) branching factor estimate b approaches or exceeds 1; (2) single-agent baseline accuracy exceeds 45%; (3) tool count exceeds 16; (4) agent count exceeds 3-4; (5) runtime communication rounds exceed 7x single-agent baseline; (6) cost trajectory shows acceleration (second derivative > 0). These are analytical/predictive triggers to be applied before or during deployment.
adaptation_cost: Architectural adaptation (switching from multi-agent to single-agent) requires restarting the task from the beginning or from the last checkpoint. Cost of adaptation = wasted computation on the abandoned topology + restart cost. The framework argues that the cost of NOT adapting (continuing with a harmful multi-agent setup) is far higher — up to 70% performance degradation and 6.2x round inflation. The predictive nature of the framework (87% accuracy) aims to minimize adaptation frequency by getting the architecture right the first time.
failure_recovery_mechanism: The primary failure recovery is architectural fallback: if multi-agent performance degrades, revert to single-agent or fewer-agent topology. The framework identifies three failure modes with specific recovery strategies: (1) Coordination collapse — reduce agent count; (2) Error amplification (17.2x in independent topology) — switch to centralized topology; (3) Capability saturation — eliminate redundant agents. Detection is via runtime metrics (rounds, cost, accuracy). No subtask-level retry or compensation — this is an architectural-level model.
output_artifacts: The framework produces: (1) task topology classification (parallelizable/sequential/exploratory); (2) single-agent baseline accuracy measurement; (3) optimal agent count recommendation (1-4); (4) optimal topology recommendation (centralized/decentralized/independent); (5) predicted branching factor b; (6) predicted cost scaling curve; (7) risk score based on distance from critical thresholds. Supporting artifacts: experimental logs (Battala), benchmark results across 180 configurations (Google/MIT).
briefing_format: The natural briefing format is a decision matrix: task type + single-agent accuracy + recommended agent count + recommended topology + predicted cost scaling + risk level. The Google/MIT paper presents results as regression plots (accuracy vs. agent count, rounds vs. agent count) with clear threshold lines. For practitioners, a one-page decision checklist: 'Is single-agent >45%? If yes, stop. Is tool count >16? If yes, use single agent. Is task sequential? If yes, use single agent. Otherwise, use 2-4 agents with centralized topology.'
traceability: Strong analytical traceability: every recommendation traces back to specific empirical measurements (accuracy, rounds, cost) or mathematical derivations (branching factor, scaling exponent). The 87% prediction accuracy is reported with confidence intervals. However, the rationale for WHY a particular task exhibits a particular topology is domain-expert-driven, not automated — this limits traceability for novel tasks. Decision rationale (why 3 agents vs. 4) is quantitative but the task classification step is qualitative.
agent_communication_protocol: Agents in the framework's empirical studies used MCP-based or custom tool-calling protocols. The framework itself is protocol-agnostic but the communication overhead analysis (n^2 links, exponent ~1.7 round growth) applies to any synchronous request-response protocol. The framework recommends minimizing communication protocol overhead: for parallelizable tasks, batch communication; for sequential tasks, the protocol overhead is inherent and the task should not be multi-agent. A2A and ANP are cited as relevant implementations.
learning_memory_architecture: No built-in learning mechanism in the framework itself. However, the Google/MIT paper notes that the scaling laws could be refined per-organization through empirical measurement: organizations should collect their own single-agent baselines, task topology classifications, and multi-agent performance data to calibrate the thresholds (45%, 16 tools, 3-4 agents) to their specific contexts. The Battala repo includes scripts for measuring branching factor from execution traces, enabling self-calibration.
latency_bounds: The framework focuses on cost and accuracy, not latency. However, the communication round analysis has latency implications: single-agent average 7.2 rounds vs. multi-agent 44.3 rounds (6.2x more). For interactive applications with latency budgets, multi-agent architectures may violate latency requirements even before violating cost budgets. The framework can be extended with latency bounds: if the predicted round count * round_time > latency_budget, fall back to single-agent.
evaluation_benchmarking: Battala evaluated on custom agentic compute chains (recursive and agentic modes vs. baseline) measuring operations, compute units, and amplification factors. Google/MIT evaluated across 180 controlled configurations: 3 LLM families (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5, Gemini 2.0), 5 architectures (single, independent, decentralized, centralized, mixed), 4 benchmarks (SWE-bench Verified, WebArena, FinanceBench, Minecraft). Metrics: accuracy, Fix Rate, communication rounds, token usage, cost. The 87% prediction accuracy is the headline validation metric across all 180 configurations.
mapping_strength: Exceptionally strong mapping to the JORDAN pipeline. The framework directly informs the PLAN node's architecture decisions: how many agents? what topology? what's the predicted cost? The branching factor model can be applied to JORDAN's task decomposition: if a JORDAN plan node proposes a task with b > 1, the risk_assessment node should flag and reject it. The 45% Rule maps directly to agent specialization decisions — if the base JORDAN agent already handles a task adequately, do not create a specialist. The topology analysis maps to the execute node's dispatch strategy.
gaps: Key gaps for JORDAN: (1) The framework predicts WHEN multi-agent will fail but not HOW to redesign the decomposition to fix it; (2) thresholds (45%, 16 tools, 3-4 agents) are derived from 2025-era LLMs and may shift with model improvements — JORDAN needs to recalibrate; (3) the framework treats tasks as monolithic — it does not address hybrid tasks where some sub-tasks are parallelizable and others sequential; (4) the 87% prediction accuracy means 13% of tasks receive incorrect architecture recommendations; (5) no integration with runtime monitoring — the framework is static/predictive; (6) the Battala branching factor model needs validation on real-world agent tasks beyond synthetic recursive chains.
adaptation_required: For JORDAN: (1) Integrate the branching factor estimator into the plan node — before committing to a decomposition, compute b for the proposed DAG and reject if b >= 1; (2) Add the 45% Rule as a gate in the risk_assessment node — check whether a single JORDAN agent can already handle a sub-task adequately before spawning specialists; (3) Implement runtime monitoring of communication rounds and cost in the execute node, with auto-fallback to simpler architecture if thresholds are breached; (4) Maintain a per-organization calibration table mapping task types to optimal agent counts, updated from JORDAN's own execution history; (5) Use topology classification to determine the execute node's dispatch strategy: centralized (manager-review) for parallelizable sub-tasks, single-agent for sequential chains; (6) Cap JORDAN agent teams at 4 per sub-task unless empirical data justifies more.
validation_context: Battala: empirical experiments on custom agentic compute chains (recursive and agentic modes), published on GitHub with Zenodo DOI. Google/MIT: 180 controlled configurations across 3 LLM families, 5 architectures, 4 benchmarks, peer-reviewed at arXiv (2512.08296, December 2025). Reported 87% prediction accuracy. Coverage includes software engineering (SWE-bench), web browsing (WebArena), finance (FinanceBench), and open-ended planning (Minecraft). Not yet validated in production agent systems or non-English contexts.
known_failures: Documented failure modes: (1) The 45% Rule fails for hybrid tasks where some sub-tasks are above 45% and others below — the framework gives a single recommendation; (2) 13% false prediction rate across the 180 configurations — some tasks receive incorrect architecture recommendations; (3) For exploratory tasks, the framework weakly predicts outcomes (+9.2% gain with high variance), making it unreliable for this task class; (4) The branching factor model assumes i.i.d. sub-task costs, which breaks when sub-tasks have highly variable complexity; (5) Thresholds are model-family-specific — Claude 3.5's optimal agent count differs from GPT-4o's; (6) The framework does not account for agent memory/context persistence — tasks where context accumulates across agents may degrade differently than predicted; (7) Framework tested only on English-language tasks.
where_still_used: The Battala framework is available on GitHub (github.com/Siva2015143/AI-Agentic-Compute-Criticality) with code, documentation, and experiment scripts. The Google/MIT paper is published on arXiv with code and evaluation harness. As of mid-2025, both are primarily research artifacts. Adoption is emerging in AI agent platform companies (LangChain/LangGraph cites similar principles in their v1.0 supervisor library guidance). The 3-4 agent ceiling and 45% Rule are circulating as design heuristics in agent engineering communities (LangChain forum, r/LocalLLaMA, AI agent Discord servers). Formal integration into production agent frameworks is expected through 2026.
